confirmed.title: importTasks: task created but subtasks written outside transaction — partial failure leaves orphaned task row
confirmed.description: In importTasks(), each task is created with prisma.task.create() and then its subtasks are created in a sequential loop of individual prisma.subtask.create() calls. There is no wrapping $transaction. If any subtask creation fails (e.g. DB constraint violation, connection interruption), the parent task row already exists in the database and will NOT be rolled back. The outer catch only records the error in errorDetails and increments result.errors — the dangling task row with no subtasks remains committed. Additionally, the duplicate-title check at line 1389 is a read followed by a create (non-atomic), creating a TOCTOU race.
confirmed.root_cause: No database transaction wraps the task.create + subtask.create sequence, so failure between them leaves partially-written data.
confirmed.impact: Orphaned task rows in the DB (no subtasks despite CSV specifying them); repeated imports will report the title as duplicate and skip it; data integrity inconsistency visible to end users.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Wrap the entire per-task block (task.create + all subtask.create) in a prisma.$transaction(). Move the duplicate check inside the transaction using upsert semantics or rely on a DB unique constraint to avoid the TOCTOU gap.
acceptance_criteria: If subtask creation throws, the parent task row is rolled back
Concurrent imports with the same title do not both succeed
confirmed.notes: validateImport() (dry-run) does not have this issue as it does not write. Verified at lines 1451-1482: no $transaction wrapping.
confirmed.adversarial_check: Confirmed. Lines 1451-1482 show prisma.task.create() followed by individual prisma.subtask.create() calls in a for-loop, with no $transaction wrapper. The catch block at line 1485 catches per-task errors but cannot roll back the already-committed task.create.

confirmed.title: addDependency: check-then-create pattern is not race-safe — duplicate dependency race between circular-check and create
confirmed.description: The circular-dependency check (checkCircularDependency) traverses the graph with a series of individual SELECT queries outside any transaction. Between the final check and the INSERT, another concurrent request can insert the reverse dependency, creating a cycle that neither check catches. The existing-dependency check is similarly non-atomic. While the DB unique constraint on (taskId, dependsOnTaskId) will prevent true duplicates, the circular check has no DB-level enforcement and can be raced by two simultaneous symmetric requests (A→B and B→A submitted concurrently).
confirmed.root_cause: The multi-step graph traversal + insert sequence has no serialization, allowing concurrent symmetric dependency creation to slip through the circular check.
confirmed.impact: Circular dependency cycle in the task graph; all graph traversals or rendering logic that follow dependency chains may enter infinite loops or produce incorrect critical-path calculations.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Wrap the circular-check + insert in a SERIALIZABLE transaction or use an advisory lock keyed on the lower/upper task UUIDs. Alternatively add a DB-level trigger or deferred constraint to enforce DAG invariant.
acceptance_criteria: Concurrent POST /:id/dependencies with reverse direction is rejected for at least one request
No cycle is observable in task dependency graph after concurrent inserts
confirmed.notes: checkCircularDependency itself is BFS, correct for sequential execution.
confirmed.adversarial_check: Confirmed. Schema has @@unique([taskId, dependsOnTaskId]) which prevents exact duplicate inserts (P2002), but circular race (A→B concurrent with B→A) has no DB-level guard. Both requests see a cycle-free graph, pass the check, then both succeed creating a cycle.

confirmed.title: reorderSubtasks: subtask IDs from payload not validated to belong to taskId — cross-task position manipulation possible
confirmed.description: reorderSubtasks() verifies that the parent task exists, but does not verify that each subtaskId in the payload actually belongs to that task (i.e., subtask.taskId === taskId). An authenticated user with tasks:update permission can send subtask IDs from a different task and silently overwrite position values on subtasks they should have no access to. The getSubtasks(taskId) result returned will be correct for the requested task but the foreign subtasks are mutated.
confirmed.root_cause: The $transaction only filters by subtask PK (id), not by the composite (id, taskId), so it operates on any subtask regardless of ownership.
confirmed.impact: Position data corruption on subtasks of arbitrary tasks; potential information-leakage side-channel (update succeeds vs 404 reveals existence of subtask IDs across task boundaries).
confirmed.suggested_fix: Either (a) pre-validate that all subtaskIds belong to taskId via a findMany({where:{id:{in:subtaskIds}, taskId}}) length check, or (b) change each update where clause to {id, taskId} so unowned subtasks produce a P2025 not-found error.
acceptance_criteria: Sending a subtaskId belonging to a different task returns 404 or 400
Position of subtasks on the other task is unmodified
confirmed.notes: updateSubtask and deleteSubtask correctly use findFirst({where:{id, taskId}}) — reorderSubtasks diverges from that pattern. Verified at lines 2006-2007 and 2029-2030.
confirmed.adversarial_check: Confirmed. updateSubtask (line 2006) and deleteSubtask (line 2029) both use findFirst({where:{id:subtaskId, taskId}}) before acting. reorderSubtasks (line 2051-2057) uses where:{id} only, no taskId filter. Cross-task mutation is possible.

confirmed.title: importTasks: missing date validity check — invalid startDate/endDate strings silently stored as Invalid Date
confirmed.code_evidence:
startDate: taskData.startDate ? new Date(taskData.startDate) : null,
            endDate: taskData.endDate ? new Date(taskData.endDate) : null,
confirmed.description: In importTasks() (the actual write path, distinct from validateImport()), dates are passed directly to `new Date()` without validating the result with isNaN(). If taskData.startDate is a non-ISO string that the Date constructor cannot parse, `new Date(taskData.startDate)` returns an Invalid Date object, which Prisma will attempt to pass to PostgreSQL. The corresponding validateImport() path validates dates (lines 1653-1673), but only when BOTH startDate and endDate are present (condition: `if (taskData.startDate && taskData.endDate)`). The actual write path has no validation at all. DTO field startDate/endDate use @IsString() not @IsDateString().
confirmed.root_cause: Date validation is only performed in the dry-run path (validateImport), not in the real write path (importTasks). Additionally validateImport only validates when both dates are present.
confirmed.impact: Tasks with invalid dates will cause a Prisma/DB error caught by the per-row catch block — the task won't be created silently with corrupt dates, but the error message will be a cryptic Prisma error rather than a user-friendly validation message.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Add `isNaN(new Date(taskData.startDate).getTime())` guards in importTasks() before constructing Date objects, consistent with the pattern in validateImport() lines 1655-1669.
acceptance_criteria: POST /project/:projectId/import with an invalid startDate returns an error for that row, not a task with null/invalid date
confirmed.notes: The ImportTaskDto uses @IsString() not @IsDateString() for startDate/endDate (verified). Impact is slightly softened: Prisma will throw on Invalid Date, caught by per-row catch, so no silent data corruption — but error message is cryptic.
confirmed.adversarial_check: Confirmed. Lines 1461-1462 verified: no isNaN guards. ImportTaskDto at startDate/endDate fields uses @IsString() @IsOptional() (no @IsDateString()). validateImport at line 1652 only validates when both dates exist. Write path has zero date validation.

confirmed.title: update(): date clearing is impossible — falsy check prevents clearing startDate/endDate to null
confirmed.code_evidence:
...(startDate && { startDate: new Date(startDate) }),
          ...(endDate && { endDate: new Date(endDate) }),
confirmed.description: In update(), dates are set only when the value is truthy. However, the UpdateTaskDto fields are optional strings — if a client sends `startDate: null` or an explicit empty string to clear a date, the falsy check skips the field entirely and the existing date is retained. There is no way to clear a date via PATCH. This is a business-logic correctness gap: date clearing is a valid user action (removing a deadline). The same pattern exists for create() lines 233-234 but create context is less critical since you can just omit the field.
confirmed.root_cause: Using a falsy guard (`...(startDate && {...})`) instead of an explicit `!== undefined` check; null/empty-string inputs are silently ignored.
confirmed.impact: Once a startDate or endDate is set on a task, it cannot be cleared via the PATCH API; the UI or any client that sends null to remove a date will receive a stale non-null date in the response.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Change to `...(startDate !== undefined && { startDate: startDate ? new Date(startDate) : null })` so explicit null/empty string clears the field.
acceptance_criteria: PATCH /tasks/:id with {endDate: null} results in task.endDate being null
PATCH /tasks/:id without endDate key leaves endDate unchanged
confirmed.notes: projectId clearing uses `...(projectId && {projectId})` with the same issue (line 789) but that case is handled by detachFromProject endpoint.
confirmed.adversarial_check: Confirmed. Lines 795-796 verified verbatim: `...(startDate && { startDate: new Date(startDate) })` and `...(endDate && { endDate: new Date(endDate) })`. No undefined check. Null/empty string inputs skip the field entirely.

confirmed.title: assertTeleworkCompatibility: date comparison uses naive new Date(string) — TZ ambiguity with date-only strings
confirmed.description: The dates array received from the DTO contains ISO date strings. These are passed through `new Date(date)` and used in an `IN` filter against the DB `date` column (@db.Date). The TeleworkSchedule.date is a PostgreSQL DATE type. If date-only strings like '2026-03-25' are passed (which are valid @IsDateString() inputs), new Date('2026-03-25') in Node.js parses as midnight UTC — which happens to be correct for UTC servers. However, occurrence-generator.ts uses Date.UTC() strictly, creating an inconsistency in convention. On non-UTC deployments, date-only strings would produce wrong dates.
confirmed.root_cause: Inconsistent date construction: occurrence-generator uses Date.UTC() strictly; assertTeleworkCompatibility uses new Date(string) which is TZ-sensitive for date-only strings.
confirmed.impact: On non-UTC server deployments or when date strings lack time component, the telework conflict check returns false negatives, allowing telework-incompatible assignments to be created. Low risk on current UTC prod deployment.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Normalize date strings to midnight UTC before querying: `dates.map(d => { const p = d.split('T')[0]; const [y,m,day] = p.split('-').map(Number); return new Date(Date.UTC(y, m-1, day)); })` — matching the occurrence-generator convention.
acceptance_criteria: Telework conflict check correctly detects conflicts on any server TZ setting
confirmed.notes: Prod is UTC (MEMORY.md), TeleworkSchedule.date is @db.Date. BulkAssignmentDto uses @IsDateString() with UTC-qualified examples. Risk is latent but real for date-only inputs or non-UTC deployments.
confirmed.adversarial_check: Confirmed at reduced severity. Code at line 35 verified: `dates.map((date) => new Date(date))`. Schema confirmed @db.Date on TeleworkSchedule.date. BulkAssignmentDto allows any @IsDateString() input including date-only strings. occurrence-generator uses Date.UTC() strictly. Inconsistency is real; current prod exposure is low (UTC server + UTC-qualified inputs in examples).

confirmed.title: createBulkAssignment: telework check snapshot race — assertTeleworkCompatibility runs before the bulk inserts, but concurrent mutations can invalidate it
confirmed.description: The telework compatibility check is performed once as a snapshot before the loop starts. The bulk insert loop then runs N×M individual creates outside any transaction. Between the check and the insert loop (or between any two iterations of the loop), a concurrent request could change a user's telework schedule for one of the dates. More importantly, if the bulk insert itself takes seconds (large N×M), the check is stale. There is no transactional or re-check mechanism.
confirmed.root_cause: assertTeleworkCompatibility is an upfront read with no lock, and the subsequent writes are not in a transaction, creating a TOCTOU window.
confirmed.impact: Telework-incompatible assignments can slip through during concurrent schedule changes; for large bulk operations the window is proportionally large.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Either wrap the entire operation in a REPEATABLE READ transaction and re-validate inside, or re-run the telework check per-assignment pair inside the loop before creating.
acceptance_criteria: A concurrent telework schedule update during bulk assignment is detected and the conflicting assignment is rejected
confirmed.notes: createAssignment (single) has the same pattern but the window is much smaller.
confirmed.adversarial_check: Confirmed. Lines 288-334 verified: assertTeleworkCompatibility called once before loop at line 298, then N×M predefinedTaskAssignment.create calls in nested for-loops with no transaction wrapper. The TOCTOU window exists.

confirmed.title: update(): progress not recalculated when status changes and subtasks exist
confirmed.description: When a task has subtasks, update() deliberately skips updating progress (the condition `=== 0` is false). This is consistent with the design choice that progress is driven by subtask completion when subtasks exist. However, if the task has subtasks AND the caller explicitly sets `status: DONE`, the task is marked as DONE but progress remains at whatever partial value it had. A task can therefore be DONE at 40% progress which is contradictory. recalcTaskProgress() handles the subtask path, but it is only called from subtask create/update/delete — not from task status update.
confirmed.root_cause: Progress update when status changes is skipped entirely if subtasks exist; status=DONE with partial subtask completion creates an inconsistent state.
confirmed.impact: Task can appear as DONE with progress < 100% in reports and dashboards; inconsistent data for any analytics that combines status + progress.
confirmed.suggested_fix: When status is set to DONE, override progress to 100 regardless of subtask count, OR prevent setting DONE when not all subtasks are completed. Add a check: `if (taskData.status === TaskStatus.DONE) { progress = 100; }` and apply unconditionally.
acceptance_criteria: Setting a task with subtasks to status=DONE results in progress=100
Setting status=IN_PROGRESS with subtasks keeps progress driven by subtask completion
confirmed.notes: recalcTaskProgress at line 2063 exits early if subtasks.length === 0, so it would not fix this if called from update().
confirmed.adversarial_check: Confirmed. Lines 797-800 verified: progress only updated when subtask count === 0. recalcTaskProgress (line 2063-2074) is not called from update(). task_progress.helper.ts maps DONE→100, but this path is bypassed when subtasks exist. Status=DONE with partial subtasks creates inconsistency.

confirmed.title: findAll: default limit is 1000 but page default is 1 — no hard cap when limit param is omitted via service default
confirmed.description: When limit is not provided (undefined), `limit || 1000` evaluates to 1000 and safeLimit is capped at 1000. This is intentional but means any call to findAll() without a limit returns up to 1000 tasks. Combined with the fact that the controller does not enforce a default limit (it passes undefined if not provided), a caller can retrieve 1000 tasks in one request. The findForPlanningOverview path applies a hard cap of 500 but findAll does not have such a comment explaining the 1000 choice. This is a minor concern — not incorrect per se, but the inconsistency between 500 (planning) and 1000 (findAll) warrants documenting.
confirmed.root_cause: The two query paths use different hard caps without explanation; findAll cap of 1000 is implicit default, not a named constant.
confirmed.impact: Large result sets on findAll can cause performance issues in large deployments; inconsistent cap between endpoints.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Define a named constant TASKS_HARD_CAP = 1000 (analogous to PLANNING_HARD_CAP = 500), document why different caps exist, and ensure the default is applied at the controller layer too.
acceptance_criteria: GET /tasks without limit param returns at most 1000 results
confirmed.notes: comments/comments.service.ts has the same pattern with default 1000.
confirmed.adversarial_check: Confirmed. Line 305 verified verbatim: `const safeLimit = Math.min(limit || 1000, 1000);`. The cap is enforced (1000 max), but the inconsistency with the 500 planning cap and the implicit default behavior are real.
